Constructive support for offer preparation

Background

The investigation identified insufficient welded connections within the LV copper bus ducts, which are composed of multiple copper flat conductors. The findings indicate potential risks, including:

Reduced effective conductor cross-section at weld locations

Increased electrical resistance due to poor or inconsistent weld quality

Potential loosening or degradation of electrical contact over time

Elevated risk of localized overheating and long-term reliability issues

These risks may negatively impact electrical performance, thermal behavior, and overall system reliability.

Engineering Demand

We formally request a design change from welded busbar connections to crimped connections, ensuring:

Stable and repeatable electrical contact

Improved mechanical robustness

Lower contact resistance

Enhanced long-term reliability under thermal cycling and operational loads

The proposed crimped connection shall be designed, validated, and qualified according to applicable standards and internal engineering guidelines.

Requested Actions

Engineering evaluation of the proposed crimped busbar connection design

Definition of applicable crimping method, tooling, and quality controls

Electrical and thermal validation (including resistance and temperature rise)

Assessment of manufacturability and implementation timeline

Please provide your technical assessment, feasibility, and proposed next steps at your earliest convenience.

Bushing Exchange  on two-phase shifters.
All relevant information is in Fabasoft s.261078. 
Here is one problem that needs your attention.
In the tender documentation, the Customer demands a change to the existing leads.

The existing two M12 cable lugs (for two 185 mm² cables) are rated for currents up to 2 x 800 A = 1600 A.
Depending on the bushing type and the manufacturer of the lug socket (oil-filled) at the winding output, the connection must be adjusted accordingly.

From my point of view the bottleneck is the two 185 mm² copper cables. For a current of 2000 A, four 185 mm² copper cables would be required.

The problem is that we would have to implement this solution on-site.

My theoretical solution is:

Have the copper conduit cut and crimp new cables together, but this carries the risk of contaminating the transformer.

Description:
Die beiden PSTs in UW Diele (Ser. No; 1,726.622/3, wo.r: 66732/3 bzw. Ser. No; 1,726.624/5, wo.r: 66734/5)
aus den Baujahren 2006/7 sollen im Zuge der UW-Erweiterung um 2 weitere PSTs auf neue Fundamente
versetzt werden. Die Ausschreibung für die neuen PSTs läuft gerade.
D.h., die Bestands-PSTs sollen möglichst auf die identischen Fundamente wie die aktuellen PSTs für
Waldeck versetzt werden.
Da diese älteren PSTs mit ODAN -Kühlung und 2 Radiatorenbatterien je Kessel ausgeführt wurden, geht
diese Umsetzung nur mit dem Anbau von einer neuen, kleineren ODAF-Radiatorenbatterie.
Erschwerend kommt noch hinzu, dass bei diesen PST entgegen unserem neuen Standard die Kühler nicht auf der Schalterseite der Kessel angeschlossen sind.
Folgende Fragen habe ich hier aktuell:
- Sind die Blindflansche unten auf der Schalterseite mit den internen OD-Kanälen verbunden?
Könnte die Kaltölleitung neuer Kühler hier angeschlossen werden?

Könnte der Warmölanschluss mit vertretbaren Aufwand nach vorne auf die Schalterseite mittels
externen Rohrleitungen (möglichst nur eine je Kessel) neben/über dem Kesseldeckel verlegt werden?

Wäre es möglich die vorhandenen ODAN-Radiatorenbatterien durch die ODAF-Radiatorenbatterien
der aktuellen PSTs zu ersetzen?
Die Verlustleitung der Bestands-PSTs (0,340 MW + 2,13 MW=2,47 MW ) ist etwas kleiner als die
Verluste bei den aktuellen PSTs( 0,25 MW+2,45 MW=2,7 MW).
Könnte auch eine Pumpe PR300/6 die beiden aktuell installierten Pumpen PR250/6 ersetzen?
Eine PR300/6 in Kaltölleitung möglich?

Request_ID: SF 262543603 - Alliant Energy - TP499/500 TR Uprating
Description:
Request Summary:
We request an estimate of engineering hours to perform an engineering assessment for transformer uprating of two (02) GSU units.
The intention is to prepare a customer offer covering only the engineering study and the final report.

Transformer Data

Quantity: 2 units
Type: Three-phase GSU Transformer
Rating: 150 / 200 / 250 MVA
Voltage: 142 / 18 kV
Cooling: ONAN / ONAF / ONAF
Tap Changer: DETC
OEM: Siemens Energy Mexico (legacy Ferranti Packard)

End Customer
Alliant Energy – Riverside Plant
Background
The customer is evaluating the possibility of uprating the current GSUs to support increased generation output (target up to 290 MVA).
Some pictures and limited documentation of the transformers in operation were provided.

Scope of Work:
Engineering assessment to evaluate the feasibility of uprating the two GSU transformers.
Expected Output (for reference only – not required at this stage)
Although not required in detail for this hours estimate, the engineering assessment is expected to include:

Thermal evaluation (top oil temperature extrapolation based on current and proposed losses) - Test Report Available.
Verification of current-carrying capacity of critical components (Bushings, CT's, DETC, etc)
Preliminary recommendations (e.g., cooling upgrades such as additional fans or fans replacements)
Transformer life expectancy impact under higher loading
Conceptual sketches (croquis) showing mainly modification.
Technical conclusion regarding uprating feasibility

Important Note
At this stage, we only require the estimated engineering hours to perform the assessment described above.
Preparation of a tender for spare parts

Customer inquiry:
1. Mesh-dome transformer (200 MVA, ELIN, TDQ-365R27T9K-99), manufactured in 1999,
serial number 1690028
This transformer is equipped with Euromold connectors on System 4 (20 kV) (Euromold Series 200, Type
K180AR-2/J).
We would like to replace these with Pfisterer connectors, e.g., Pfisterer Connex single
angle connectors, size 1. These are installed in another power distribution transformer of the same type
(SIEMENS, TDQ-365R27T9K-99, manufactured in 2013).  
Do you know if this is possible and permissible? The Pfisterer connector with the
correct cone length would have to be used (A = 168)
Or are there still replacement plugs from Euromold that would fit?
For this transformer, we would also swap out the bushings from System 1 and System 2.
These bushings are all from HSP (SETFt ...).
Would it be advisable to use the same bushings (if available) from HSP
again in this case?

For other transformers, we have also used suitable bushings from Moser Glaser
in the past.  
A bushing replacement is also pending for three other 450 MVA transformers. To keep things
clear, however, I am referring here only to the 200 MVA power coupler mentioned above.
These projects are planned for 2028. Do you offer such services as part of your transformer maintenance program? That is, bushing replacement?

2. The serial numbers of our three 450 MVA grid-coupling transformers are as follows:
Two are from ELIN and are of the same type (TDQ-405R44D9K-99): 1632330 and 1632331
The third is from SCHORCH (now GE?): Type TC 2456E, 627150101
For these three transformers as well, we have installed the same Euromold connectors (Type K180AR-2/J)
on System 4.
And the bushings (Systems 1 and 2) are all from HSP (SOTF, SETF).
I first need to clarify internally and with the company how we will handle the maintenance of our transformers
here—that is, whether we will simply replace the bushings or focus more on monitoring.
We perform diagnostic measurements every 5 years.
Therefore, before you invest time in detailed planning or preparing a quote, a rough cost estimate would be
more than sufficient for us during the current decision-making and budgeting phase.
For budgeting purposes, a non-binding cost estimate for replacing the bushings would be very
helpful—specifically for replacing the bushings in Systems 1, 2, and 4 of the relevant
transformers.
